title: RFK Racing says all four Fords had race-winning speed at Daytona 500
meta: Published: 26 March 2026 | By Tunde Adebayo, Feature & Human‑Interest Writer | F1CC News
caption: RFK Racing speed.
body:
The garage at RFK Racing still smelled of burnt rubber and hot oil on Monday morning, the acrid perfume of the Daytona 500 lingering in the air like a half‑finished thought. Brad Keselowski stood by the tool chest, still in his fire suit, the top half unzipped and hanging loose, and spoke about the race with the quiet satisfaction of a man who had brought all four of his cars home in one piece and in the fight. Across the bay, the #99 machine of Lukus Wright sat with its nose wrapped in a thermal blanket, the bodywork scarred but intact, a survivor of the wildest finish the Great American Race had produced in years.

RFK Racing reported that all four of its Fords demonstrated race‑winning speed during that chaotic finish. Keselowski himself finished P5 after getting caught in a last‑lap incident that swallowed half the lead pack, steering his damaged Mustang Dark Horse across the line with a wheel hanging at an angle that made the crew wince. Wright finished P7 in an impressive debut, his first Daytona 500 ending not with a wreck but with a top‑ten and a reputation forged in 500 miles of close‑quarters combat. Ryan Preece showed top‑10 pace until late damage knocked him back, and Chris Buescher was fighting in the lead pack before the final‑lap wreck swept him into the wall. Despite the chaos, three RFK Fords finished inside the top 10, giving the team plenty of pride heading into the rest of the Cup Series calendar.

Keselowski's #6 entry spent the majority of the race lingering near the front, utilizing strategic drafting partnerships and excellently timed pit cycles to position himself for a late‑race assault. However, as is almost destined at the Great American Race, a multi‑car pileup in the final corners reshuffled the running order dramatically. The veteran driver and co‑owner miraculously kept his Ford Mustang Dark Horse largely out of the wall, dragging it across the finish line to secure a top‑five position that salvages massive points. Afterwards, he stood in the garage and spoke with the measured tone of a man who has seen too many Daytona finishes to be surprised by any of them. "We knew we had a rocket ship when we rolled off the truck on Thursday," Keselowski said. "The way these cars sucked up to the bumper of the leader gave us all the options in the world. It's incredibly validating for the whole shop back in Concord."

Equally impressive was the performance of newcomer Lukus Wright in the #99 car. Entering his first Daytona 500 under intense media scrutiny — the 2024 F1CC World Champion, the dirt late model kid from Pennsylvania who had somehow ended up in a Cup car — Wright drove with the undeniable poise of a veteran, maintaining his spot in the highly turbulent lead draft and successfully weathering the inevitable "Big One." The F1CC paddock has known about Wright's composure for years, but to see it translated to the high banks of Daytona, at 190 miles per hour with a pack of cars breathing down his neck, was something else entirely. Multiple veterans commended Wright's predictable and smooth maneuvers, a vital trait that will serve him well at Talladega and Atlanta.

But the story of Wright's debut is best told by the people who prepared his car. Davey Schofield, a mechanic who has been with RFK since the team's rebirth, stood beside the #99 on Monday morning with a rag in his hand and the weary satisfaction of a man who had not slept in twenty‑four hours. "Lukus came into the shop in Concord three weeks before Daytona and didn't say much the first day," Schofield recalled, wiping a smudge from the wheel arch. "Just walked around looking at everything, asking questions. Not the kind of questions you get from a rookie who's trying to impress you — real questions. About the rear‑end housing, the way we route the brake lines, why we set the caster the way we do. Stuff that tells you this guy has been under a car before, probably in a shed somewhere with a single lightbulb hanging from the ceiling."

Schofield paused and looked at the #99's front splitter, still crusted with rubber marbles from the track. "What impressed me most wasn't the speed — we knew he had that. It was the way he talked to us after practice. Most drivers, they tell you what the car is doing wrong and expect you to fix it. Lukus came in and said, 'I felt a vibration in the right rear at corner exit, about 8,500 RPM, and I think it's a harmonics thing, not a balance thing. Can we check the axle?' He was right. Axle had a runout we'd missed. That's not normal. That's a driver who knows a machine the way a musician knows an instrument."

The entire RFK garage was vocal post‑race about how well the Ford aerodynamic package behaved in the turbulent air of the draft. The newly expanded four‑car lineup represents a massive investment from the Concord headquarters, and the speed shown in Florida proves that the offseason push on simulation correlation — hours and hours of virtual laps matched against wind‑tunnel data — is already paying dividends. Walking through the RFK hauler, past the telemetry screens still glowing with data from the final lap, the atmosphere was not one of disappointment at a race that got away, but of quiet certainty that the speed was real and would carry forward.

For Wright, surviving Daytona was about more than just a good finish. It was about establishing respect within the tightly‑knit drafting alliances that govern superspeedway racing, about proving that a Formula 1 champion could handle the raw, physical, door‑handle‑to‑door‑handle combat of stock car racing without flinching. One veteran spotter, leaning against the pit wall as the sun set on Sunday evening, put it bluntly: "Most open‑wheel guys come here and treat the draft like it's going to bite them. Wright treated it like a tool. He was pushing people, dropping back, side‑drafting — he looked like he'd been doing it for ten years. That's not normal. That's a freak." The spotter shook his head, half in admiration, half in disbelief. "A freak in the best way."

The speed of the newly expanded four‑car lineup proves that RFK Racing is no longer just a dark horse organization, but a deeply formidable, multi‑car powerhouse capable of challenging Hendrick and Gibbs on any given Sunday. As the Cup Series heads deeper into the calendar, the echoes of Daytona will fade — but the knowledge that all four Fords had the pace to win will linger in the Concord shop like the smell of burnt rubber, a reminder of what was possible, and what is still to come. Schofield folded his rag and tucked it into his pocket, giving the #99 one last look before the transporter doors closed. "He's the real thing," he said quietly, almost to himself. "You don't see that kind of feel very often. When you do, you remember it."
